notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your recent financial activity reflects a successful consulting practice, with $13,960 in deposits last month from clients like Figma and Notion. Since you live alone in Austin and have no dependents, your insurance strategy shifts away from traditional "family protection" toward "income and asset preservation"—ensuring that a single event doesn't derail the professional momentum you've built.
Since insurance decisions involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a licensed insurance professional for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Disability and income protection
As a solo consultant, your primary financial asset is your ability to generate income. Without a secondary household income or employer-sponsored benefits to rely on, a period of illness or injury is your most significant risk.
"Own-occupation" vs. "Any-occupation": Many standard policies only pay if you cannot perform any job. For a specialized UX consultant, you typically want to evaluate "True Own-Occupation" coverage. This ensures that if you cannot specifically perform your consulting duties, you could receive benefits even if you were technically able to work in a different, lower-paying field.
Residual disability rider: Since you manage multiple clients, you might experience a partial disability where you can work part-time but your income drops. A residual rider typically pays a partial benefit if your income decreases by 20% or more due to a covered illness or injury.
Professional liability (E&O)
Your work involves high-stakes design systems and research for enterprise clients. If a client alleges that your advice or deliverables caused them a financial loss—such as a delayed product launch or a faulty user interface—Professional Indemnity (Errors and Omissions) insurance provides for your legal defense.
Contractual requirements: Enterprise clients often require specific E&O limits (commonly $1M per claim) before signing a contract.
AI-liability considerations: In 2026, many professional policies have introduced specific exclusions for "unvetted AI tools." Since you likely use advanced software in your UX workflow, it is worth verifying that your indemnity coverage extends to "technology-aided errors" to avoid coverage gaps during a claim.
Cyber liability and data security
Handling client data through platforms like Figma, Notion, and Stripe creates a digital footprint that requires protection. Standard professional liability policies often exclude data breaches or cyber-attacks.
Hard market requirements: The cyber insurance market has tightened significantly in 2026. Insurers now frequently audit "technical controls" before issuing a policy. To avoid being denied coverage, you typically need to prove you have Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) enabled on all cloud applications and use encrypted backups.
Breach response: A dedicated cyber policy doesn't just pay damages; it often provides "first-party" coverage for forensics, client notification costs, and legal fees required by state privacy laws if your systems are compromised.
Health insurance and the HSA
You are currently paying $415 monthly for a Marketplace health plan and contributed $300 to your Fidelity HSA last month. This is an excellent way to build a "triple-tax-advantaged" medical emergency fund.
The 2026 subsidy cliff: A key consideration this year is the potential expiration of expanded Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies. If your income exceeds 400% of the Federal Poverty Level, your unsubsidized premiums could rise significantly.
HSA limits: For 2026, the individual contribution limit for an HSA has increased to $4,400. Since you have no dependents, maximizing this account can serve as a secondary retirement vehicle, as funds can be used for non-medical expenses after age 65 (though they are taxed as ordinary income in that scenario).
Business equipment and equipment riders
You currently reside at Eastside Flats, and while you likely have renter's insurance, these policies often have very low limits (frequently $2,500) for "business property" kept at home.
Replacement cost: Between high-end laptops, monitors, and specialized peripherals, your setup likely exceeds standard renter's limits. You might consider an "inland marine" rider or a Business Owner's Policy (BOP) to ensure your gear is covered for its full replacement value, even if it is stolen while you are traveling for a client onsite.
Would you like to see how much more you could contribute to your HSA this year to reach the $4,400 limit? 📈
